topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a resource-frugal, adaptive quantum error correction (QEC) framework tailored for NISQ-edge quantum sensors that integrates fast classical feedback loops with dynamic code switching to minimize qubit overhead while meeting strict real-time latency and power constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Current NISQ devices suffer from noise and decoherence that severely limit error-corrected quantum computations, especially in resource-constrained edge sensing scenarios where classical post-processing and quantum circuit depth are severely limited. Traditional QEC codes and existing low-overhead methods either demand excessive qubits or introduce latency incompatible with real-time sensing. There is a critical need for an efficient QEC protocol that adapts to changing error environments with minimal resource use, enabling practical error suppression within NISQ hardware and operational constraints.

APPROACH:  
We propose an adaptive QEC scheme that dynamically switches among a small set of lightweight, low-overhead error correction codes (e.g., tailored repetition codes, small-distance surface codes, and error-detecting codes) based on real-time noise diagnostics obtained via in-situ, low-latency classical feedback. The system will employ a fast classical co-processor integrated on-chip to perform error syndrome decoding and select optimal code parameters on-the-fly, balancing between error suppression efficacy and resource consumption. Additionally, we incorporate error-aware qubit mapping and gate scheduling optimizations guided by the live noise profile to further reduce logical error rates without increasing quantum circuit depth.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted on IBM Quantum Experience and Rigetti Aspen NISQ devices, using benchmark quantum sensing datasets such as the QASMBench (Quantum Algorithm and Sensing Benchmark) and the QSED (Quantum Sensor Error Dataset). Baselines include the low-overhead QEC framework from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices” (2026), the adaptive noise-aware compiler from “Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design” (2026), and surface code implementations from “Limitations of QEC in NISQ Systems” (2026). Metrics include logical error rate reduction (>30% improvement vs baseline low-overhead QEC), latency reduction (targeting sub-10 µs syndrome decoding and feedback loop), and qubit overhead reduction (≤50% relative to baseline codes). Experiments will be repeated across 5 random seeds and ablation studies will isolate the impact of adaptive code switching and error-aware qubit mapping.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior low-overhead QEC methods that statically fix the error correction code regardless of fluctuating noise, our approach dynamically adapts error correction codes in real-time leveraging fast classical co-processing, which neither “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices” nor “Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design” achieve simultaneously under strict latency and power constraints. This enables practical, real-time error mitigation directly suited for NISQ edge quantum sensors, a capability currently unattainable by existing static or heavyweight hybrid error mitigation techniques.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop a dynamic, learning-based quantum error correction framework that integrates adaptive low-overhead QEC codes with compiler-level noise-aware gate optimization for scalable, real-time error suppression on NISQ devices under hardware drift.

BACKGROUND: Current quantum error correction (QEC) methods for NISQ devices either incur excessive qubit overhead, limiting scalability, or rely on static error mitigation techniques that fail to adapt to hardware noise fluctuations and diverse user workloads in cloud quantum computing environments. Existing low-overhead QEC codes (e.g., specialized surface codes) and error-resilient compilers lack dynamic, data-driven adaptation to evolving noise, resulting in degraded fidelity and reduced practical utility on near-term quantum hardware.

APPROACH: We propose an integrated framework combining (1) a dynamic QEC code selection and parameter tuning module based on reinforcement learning that continuously adapts low-overhead QEC codes (such as the 10-qubit low-overhead code from [3]) to observed noise characteristics at runtime, and (2) a noise-aware quantum compiler extension that optimizes qubit mapping and gate scheduling using real-time noise profiles and error syndromes. This system leverages large-scale measurement data from benchmark quantum circuits to train deep learning models that predict error trends and guide both QEC code adaptation and compiler optimizations, enabling scalable, workload-generalizable error correction with minimal overhead.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on benchmark NISQ datasets including QASMBench (quantum algorithm synthesis and benchmarking), IBM Quantum Experience workloads, and the Rigetti Quantum Cloud Services benchmark suite. Baselines include the static low-overhead QEC code from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices” (2026), error-resilient compiler from “Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design” (2026), and the static dynamic error mitigation model from “Dynamic, Scalable Error Mitigation Models for Quantum Cloud Computing” (2027). Metrics are logical error rate (target <0.005), circuit fidelity improvement (>5% absolute increase over baselines), and qubit overhead reduction (at least 30% fewer physical qubits than surface code equivalents). Experimental protocol includes 5 independent runs with varying noise profiles on IBM Quantum hardware simulators with a compute budget of 1000 GPU-hours for model training and ablation studies isolating QEC adaptation and compiler optimization effects.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work which either statically applies low-overhead QEC codes or separately focuses on compiler noise mitigation, this approach jointly and dynamically adapts code parameters and compiler strategies using reinforcement learning models trained on large-scale real-time data, enabling robust, scalable error correction that generalizes across workloads and hardware noise drift. This addresses the critical bottleneck of static, workload-specific error models and qubit overhead limitations inherent in existing QEC and compiler designs.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a Hybrid Static-Dynamic Quantum Error Correction (HS-D QEC) framework that integrates a robust static QEC core with a minimal, hardware-aware dynamic tuning layer optimized for cryogenic batch NISQ processors to maximize coherence time while strictly controlling thermal overhead and reconfiguration frequency.

BACKGROUND:  
Current adaptive error correction techniques fail on NISQ-era cryogenic quantum hardware due to the high thermal and latency costs of frequent qubit reconfiguration, which disrupt qubit coherence. Traditional static QEC codes offer stability but lack adaptability to fluctuating noise profiles, limiting performance improvements in real-world NISQ devices with slow cryogenic reconfiguration cycles. The open problem is designing an error correction paradigm that balances adaptability with the stringent hardware constraints of cryogenic NISQ systems.

APPROACH:  
HS-D QEC partitions the error correction process into a stable, low-overhead static core employing a tailored low-qubit-count code (such as the tailored low-overhead code from "Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum Devices" (2026)) combined with a minimal dynamic tuning module that adjusts a small subset of parameters (e.g., syndrome extraction timing and selective code distance tuning) at controlled frequencies ≤10 Hz to respond to slow noise fluctuations. This dynamic layer is optimized via a cryogenic-aware control scheduler that minimizes thermal load and latency, validated through emulated cryogenic hardware platforms. The static core ensures baseline error suppression, while the dynamic layer fine-tunes performance without violating thermal budgets.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate HS-D QEC on the IBM Quantum Falcon processor emulators and Rigetti Aspen cryogenic hardware emulators using the Quantum Volume (QV) benchmark (IBM QV dataset), the Qiskit Noise Benchmarking Dataset, and the Superconducting Qubit Coherence Benchmark dataset. Metrics include coherence time improvement ≥20% relative to static-only QEC, logical error rate reduction by at least 15% over baseline low-overhead codes, and thermal load measured in mW to confirm adherence to ≤5 mW thermal budget typical for dilution refrigerators. Perform 10 independent runs per configuration, with ablations isolating static-only, dynamic-only, and hybrid approaches.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either applies fully static or fully adaptive QEC without cryogenic hardware constraints, HS-D QEC explicitly integrates hardware-aware dynamic tuning restricted by real cryogenic reconfiguration limits (frequency and thermal overhead), enabling practical error correction adaptability unachievable by existing adaptive QEC methods which ignore cryogenic batch reconfiguration overhead. This solution is the first to operationalize a hybrid QEC framework validated on cryogenic emulators with strict thermal and latency constraints.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop a compact, hardware-tailored neural model for on-device, real-time quantum error mitigation in NISQ devices that dynamically learns and adapts to device-specific error patterns within strict latency and energy constraints.

BACKGROUND: NISQ devices suffer from complex, device-specific noise that traditional quantum error correction (QEC) techniques cannot efficiently handle due to high qubit overhead and runtime complexity. Current embedded quantum control approaches struggle with large model sizes and latency, making them unsuitable for real-time, autonomous error mitigation on resource-constrained quantum processors. The open problem is designing lightweight, low-latency models that accurately capture intricate error statistics without offloading computation to external systems.

APPROACH: We propose a novel embedded quantum error mitigation framework combining compact, sparsely-activated neural architectures with quantization-aware training tailored for FPGA or ASIC quantum control hardware. The model integrates a hybrid classical-quantum feedback loop that incrementally updates error statistics using low-overhead online learning, enabling adaptation to temporal noise drift. Hardware-algorithm co-design ensures the model fits within strict memory (< 1 MB) and timing (< 100 µs inference) budgets while maintaining high fidelity error correction.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on open quantum control datasets including IBM Qiskit Noise Models, Rigetti Aspen Noise Dataset, and the Quantum Error Correction Benchmark Suite (QEC-Bench). Baselines include conventional surface code QEC, Low-Overhead QEC Codes (2026), and embedded error mitigation via classical LSTM models. Metrics: achieve >15% reduction in logical error rate versus baseline codes, reduce inference latency by >50% relative to LSTM-based controllers, and maintain energy consumption below 1 mJ per inference. Experimental protocol includes 5 random seeds per device noise profile, training with a 48-hour compute budget on NVIDIA A100 GPUs, and ablation studies on model sparsity and quantization levels.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior low-overhead QEC codes that focus solely on code design and compiler optimizations that lack embedded adaptability, this work enables fully on-device, real-time learning of complex, device-specific error models within stringent hardware constraints—crucial for autonomous NISQ quantum processors without external computational support.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a novel multimodal integrative modeling framework that combines sparse, low-resolution cryo-EM and NMR experimental data with AlphaFold-generated structural priors using a Bayesian deep generative model to accurately predict large macromolecular complex structures with quantified uncertainty.

BACKGROUND:  
Current AlphaFold-based methods excel at predicting monomeric protein structures but fail to accurately model large macromolecular assemblies due to sparse, noisy experimental data and their inability to incorporate heterogeneous, low-resolution constraints from cryo-EM and NMR effectively. The open problem is how to robustly integrate diverse, incomplete experimental data with computational predictions to generate coherent, high-confidence models of large complexes, overcoming the limitations of both AlphaFold and existing integrative methods that do not fully exploit uncertainty modeling or deep generative approaches.

APPROACH:  
We propose to build a Bayesian deep generative framework that fuses sparse cryo-EM density maps, NMR distance restraints, and AlphaFold monomeric predictions by learning a joint latent representation of protein complex structure. The model will explicitly model data uncertainty and missing information, enabling iterative refinement of the complex assembly structure guided by multimodal experimental constraints. AlphaFold models will serve as informed priors, while the generative model will reconcile these with experimental data, producing an ensemble of plausible conformations with uncertainty estimates. The framework will include a novel uncertainty-aware loss function and a multistage training procedure integrating synthetic and real data.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on benchmark datasets including the Protein Data Bank (PDB) macromolecular assemblies with paired cryo-EM maps from EMDB (Electron Microscopy Data Bank), the CASP-CAPRI challenge datasets focusing on protein complexes, and the NMR Restraints Dataset from BioMagResBank (BMRB). Baselines will include AlphaFold-Multimer, Integrative Modeling Platform (IMP), and DeepTracer-LowResEnhance. Metrics will include TM-score (>0.8 target), interface RMSD reduction (>1.5 Å absolute improvement), and uncertainty calibration metrics (expected calibration error below 5%). Ablation studies will analyze the impact of each data modality and uncertainty modeling. Experiments will be run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a 2-week compute budget, using 5 random seeds for statistical significance.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods, which either fail to integrate heterogeneous low-resolution experimental data effectively or ignore uncertainty quantification, this framework explicitly models uncertainty through a Bayesian deep generative approach and tightly couples AlphaFold priors with sparse experimental constraints in a unified model. This allows for robust, interpretable modeling of large complexes that overcomes the bottleneck of sparse, noisy data, a capability neither AlphaFold-Multimer nor DeepTracer-LowResEnhance currently provide.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a domain-guided conditional diffusion framework that generates de novo protein structures explicitly constrained by multi-modal biochemical priors—including ligand binding, enzymatic activity motifs, and cellular localization signals—enabling design of functional proteins within ultra-sparse, chemically valid regions of sequence-structure space unreachable by existing generative models.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative diffusion models for de novo protein design, including the recent guided diffusion approaches, produce physically plausible structures but fail to robustly incorporate complex, multi-modal biochemical constraints such as specific active site geometries, ligand compatibility, or subcellular targeting signals. This limitation restricts their practical utility in designing functionally specialized proteins that must simultaneously satisfy chemical stability and domain-specific functional constraints embedded in sparse, rugged energy landscapes. Addressing this gap requires conditioning the generative process on diverse structural and functional priors to navigate constrained chemical spaces effectively.

APPROACH:  
We propose a conditional guided diffusion model that integrates multiple domain priors at the latent and iterative denoising stages, including structural motif embeddings from Pfam and SCOPe, ligand-binding site fingerprints from BioLiP, and subcellular localization tags from DeepLoc. The model employs a multi-objective guidance mechanism that dynamically balances these constraints using adaptive gradient modulation during the reverse diffusion process. Additionally, a physics-informed scoring function derived from Rosetta energy terms and molecular dynamics simulations is integrated to refine candidate structures on-the-fly. The resulting framework jointly models sequence-structure-function relationships enabling the generation of chemically valid, functionally relevant protein conformations within tightly constrained design spaces.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted on the following datasets:  
- **CATH 4.3** for structural domain classification and motif embeddings,  
- **BioLiP** for ligand-binding site annotations,  
- **DeepLoc** annotated proteins for localization constraints.  

Baselines include:  
- **RFdiffusion** (2022),  
- **ProtDiff** (2023),  
- **TrDesign** (2021).  

Metrics will measure:  
- Structural accuracy via TM-score (>0.75 target improvement over baselines for novel folds),  
- Functional fidelity via ligand docking affinity scores (mean ΔG improvement >1 kcal/mol),  
- Constraint satisfaction rate (percentage of generated proteins meeting all domain priors >85%).  

Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a total compute budget of ~50,000 GPU hours, performing 5 independent seeds per condition and ablation studies removing individual priors to quantify their contribution.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior diffusion-based de novo design methods that handle either unconstrained generation or single-domain constraints, our method uniquely integrates multi-modal biochemical priors during the diffusion process and couples them with physics-informed refinement, enabling high-fidelity generation of functional proteins within ultra-constrained chemical spaces—a capability unattainable by existing approaches including RFdiffusion and ProtDiff.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Develop a generative sequence-to-structure model that explicitly incorporates uncertainty quantification and few-shot learning to accurately predict 3D structures of rare and synthetic protein variants lacking evolutionary homology.

BACKGROUND: Current state-of-the-art models like AlphaFold heavily depend on multiple sequence alignments (MSAs) and evolutionary information, which are sparse or nonexistent for rare protein variants and synthetic sequences. This leads to poor predictive accuracy and unreliable confidence estimates for such proteins, limiting our ability to characterize novel functions and design synthetic proteins with desired properties. The open problem is how to enable accurate and reliable structure prediction for proteins with minimal or no evolutionary context.

APPROACH: We propose a hybrid generative framework combining a conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE) trained on paired sequence-structure data with a calibrated uncertainty quantification module based on Bayesian deep ensembles. The model leverages meta-learning techniques to enable few-shot adaptation to rare variant families by fine-tuning on limited paired examples. Crucially, it generates diverse plausible conformations instead of a single deterministic fold, with confidence scores reflecting structural uncertainty. The model integrates physicochemical constraints and coarse-grained energy functions during generation to ensure physically realistic folds.

EXPERIMENT: We will benchmark against AlphaFold2, ESMFold, and OmegaFold on rare variant and synthetic protein datasets such as the RareSeq dataset (rare natural variants with experimentally determined structures), the Synthetic Protein Dataset (SPDS), and CASP14 refinement targets. Metrics will include TM-score and RMSD for structural accuracy, Expected Calibration Error (ECE) for confidence reliability, and diversity metrics like average pairwise RMSD among generated conformations. We target >3% absolute TM-score improvement over AlphaFold2 on rare variants and ECE below 5%. Experiments will be conducted on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with training runs repeated 5 times for statistical significance and ablation studies on uncertainty modules and meta-learning components.

NOVELTY: Unlike AlphaFold2 and existing methods that rely on evolutionary information and output single deterministic predictions, this work enables reliable multi-conformational structure generation with calibrated uncertainty for proteins with sparse or no MSAs. The integration of meta-learning for few-shot adaptation to rare variants and explicit uncertainty modeling has not been addressed in prior generative sequence-to-structure approaches.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a Normalizing Flow-based Generative Framework for Joint Modeling of Intrinsically Disordered Protein (IDP) Structural Ensembles and Their Sequence-Dependent Functional Dynamics, integrating multi-modal experimental data to predict continuous, time-resolved conformational landscapes beyond static or discrete ensemble representations.

BACKGROUND:  
Current protein structure prediction methods, including AlphaFold and its derivatives, excel at predicting single static or near-native folded conformations but fail to capture the full continuous heterogeneity and dynamic structural ensembles characteristic of intrinsically disordered proteins (IDPs). Existing normalizing flow approaches model continuous IDP ensembles from sequence but do not incorporate temporal dynamics or integrate multi-modal experimental constraints, limiting their ability to predict functionally relevant conformational transitions or sequence-dependent dynamic behaviors critical to IDP biological activity.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel probabilistic generative model combining conditional normalizing flows with latent time-series modeling (e.g., continuous-time variational autoencoders or neural ODEs) to generate sequence-conditioned, temporally resolved IDP conformational ensembles. The model will incorporate multi-modal experimental data—specifically, DisProt-annotated IDP sequences with corresponding NMR chemical shifts, single-molecule FRET data, and SAXS profiles—to constrain ensemble generation and capture dynamic structural transitions. Training will leverage joint likelihood maximization over these modalities, enabling the model to learn continuous distributions of IDP conformations evolving over biologically relevant timescales from single-sequence inputs.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on DisProt (version 8.0) with paired NMR chemical shift datasets, the Protein Ensemble Database (PED) containing smFRET trajectories, and Small Angle X-ray Scattering (SAXS) data from SASBDB. Baselines include AlphaFold2, DeepFragLib, and the prior Normalizing Flow IDP ensemble model by the tournament winner. Metrics: achieve >15% reduction in ensemble RMSD spread error compared to prior flow models, >10% improvement in KL divergence to experimental distributions, and >5% increase in correlation with experimentally measured dynamic FRET efficiencies. Conduct ablation studies on temporal modeling and multi-modal data integration components. Experiments run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of ~10,000 GPU hours, using 5 random seeds per setting for statistical robustness.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior normalizing flow models that generate only static or discrete IDP ensembles, this method uniquely models continuous, time-resolved conformational distributions conditioned on sequence and integrates multiple orthogonal experimental modalities to constrain and validate dynamic ensemble predictions. This enables capturing functionally critical IDP dynamics inaccessible to AlphaFold and existing generative approaches, thus meaningfully advancing beyond the current state-of-the-art.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a comprehensive energy-aware scaling law framework that dynamically integrates heterogeneous cloud resource power-performance profiles, workload variability, and model architecture characteristics to optimize Large Language Model (LLM) training schedules for minimal energy consumption without accuracy loss.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for LLMs focus primarily on model size, compute, and data trade-offs but fail to incorporate the heterogeneous energy efficiency and variable performance of modern cloud GPUs and TPUs under realistic workloads. This gap leads to suboptimal training strategies that overlook practical energy costs and resource constraints, impeding sustainable AI development. The challenge is to explicitly model and optimize the joint interaction between model scaling, energy consumption, and heterogeneous hardware to enable energy-optimized training regimes.

APPROACH:  
We propose coupling classical scaling laws with fine-grained energy models that characterize multiple cloud hardware types (e.g., Nvidia A100, Google TPU v4, AMD MI250) across varying load conditions. Our framework models training as a constrained optimization problem balancing energy use, accuracy targets, and hardware availability, solved via multi-objective optimization to produce dynamic training schedules that specify optimal model size, batch size, and training duration per hardware type. Power profiling and workload variability are integrated using real cloud telemetry data, enabling adaptive switching between hardware during training to minimize cumulative energy. We validate using detailed power measurement tools and simulate heterogeneous cloud environments.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the C4 dataset, WikiText-103, and OpenWebText2 for pretraining LLMs with sizes ranging from 100M to 10B parameters. Baselines include conventional scaling laws without energy constraints, the “Energy-Constrained Model Scaling for Heterogeneous Cloud Resources” method (2026), and GPT-3 training curves. Metrics are total energy consumed (kWh), final validation perplexity (target <20 on WikiText-103), and normalized energy-efficiency ratio (energy per accuracy point), targeting ≥20% energy reduction at fixed perplexity versus best baseline. Experiments run on a heterogeneous cloud cluster simulating A100, TPUv4, and MI250 hardware with power meters. Ablations include hardware heterogeneity, workload variability, and model architecture impact.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods that statically couple energy and scaling laws or consider only homogeneous resources, this work explicitly integrates real-time heterogeneous hardware power-performance variability and workload dynamics into scaling laws, producing actionable energy-accuracy trade-offs and adaptive training strategies. This enables sustainable, cloud-aware LLM training that prior scaling laws and energy models cannot provide.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop data-centric, domain-adaptive scaling laws for industrial large language models that optimize model size, data quality, and targeted data curation strategies to maximize performance and robustness under severe domain shifts and limited labeled data conditions.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing scaling laws for large language models (LLMs) largely focus on general-domain data distributions and assume abundant, relatively homogeneous datasets, which do not hold in industrial settings where domain-specific proprietary data is sparse, heterogeneous, and distributionally distinct. The open problem is the absence of principled scaling laws that guide model scaling, data curation, and training strategies optimized explicitly for narrow, domain-specific industrial tasks where domain shifts and data scarcity impede generalization and cost-effective training.

APPROACH:  
We propose to characterize and quantify the interaction between model size, data quantity, and data quality (including domain relevance and diversity) in shaping LLM performance under domain shift. This involves constructing a set of controlled experiments training transformer LLMs on varied subsets of specialized industrial datasets, employing adaptive data selection and augmentation strategies to optimize information density per sample. Key components include: (1) a domain-similarity metric to guide targeted data curation, (2) a data-quality-adjusted scaling law modeling performance as a function of effective data quantity, and (3) cross-domain transferability analyses to identify robustness thresholds. This framework will enable identifying compute-optimal model/data scaling regimes customized for domain-specific industrial applications.

EXPERIMENT:  
Train and evaluate models on at least three domain-specific datasets: (1) MIMIC-III Clinical Notes (medical domain), (2) Stack Overflow Developer Q&A (technical domain), and (3) Patent Abstracts (legal/technical domain). Baselines include GPT-3 (175B), BERT-large, and Domain-Adaptive Pretraining (DAPT). Metrics are perplexity (<30 target on domain test sets), domain-specific downstream task F1 scores (e.g., clinical entity recognition, >3% absolute improvement), and robustness measured via accuracy drop under synthetic domain shifts (<5% degradation). Experiments run on 8x A100 GPUs with 3 random seeds, including ablations on data curation and model scaling.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that treats data as a monolithic variable or focuses on general-domain scaling laws, this approach explicitly models data quality and domain shift impacts to produce predictive, actionable scaling laws tailored to sparse, heterogeneous industrial datasets. It goes beyond inverse problem formulations and pretraining data size effects by incorporating domain similarity metrics and targeted data curation as integral variables in the scaling laws, filling a critical gap in deploying LLMs effectively in real-world industrial environments.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a unified, quantitative scaling law framework that explicitly models the interplay between data augmentation diversity, data quality degradation, and model size to predict and optimize training efficiency and downstream performance of large language models (LLMs) in low-resource language settings.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for LLMs largely focus on model size, dataset size, and compute, but fail to incorporate the nuanced effects of data augmentation—particularly in low-resource languages where training data is scarce and noisy. Existing augmentation-based methods such as back-translation and synonym replacement empirically improve performance but lack rigorous quantification in scaling behavior, making it unclear how augmentation impacts model efficiency and final task metrics under fixed compute budgets. This gap limits principled decision-making on augmentation strategies and resource allocation.

APPROACH:  
We propose to extend classical scaling laws by integrating explicit parameters that capture data augmentation’s dual effects: (1) increasing effective dataset diversity and (2) introducing varying degrees of noise or quality degradation. This involves modeling data quality as a continuous variable affecting loss scaling and incorporating diversity gains as an augmentation multiplier within the scaling law formula. We will operationalize augmentation diversity and quality via novel embedding-space measures and noise estimations, and jointly fit these parameters on low-resource corpora augmented with controlled back-translation and synonym replacement intensities. The framework predicts BLEU and cross-entropy metrics as a function of model size, compute, and augmentation parameters, enabling optimization over augmentation strategies for fixed compute budgets.

EXPERIMENT:  
Validate on three low-resource language datasets: Masakhane English–Swahili, Flores-101 Swahili–English, and IARPA-BABEL languages (e.g., Pashto). Baselines include mBART50, M2M-100, and MarianMT models finetuned without augmentation. Metrics used are BLEU (target >10% absolute gain over unaugmented baselines), perplexity (target reduction >15%), and COMET (target improvement >0.05). Experiments run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a fixed compute budget equivalent to 2,000 GPU hours. Ablations will vary augmentation intensity (none, low, medium, high) and model size (100M, 500M, 1B parameters) across 3 seeds each to statistically validate model predictions.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior scaling laws focusing on raw data and model size, our method quantitatively integrates data augmentation’s competing effects on data quality and diversity into a single predictive scaling framework. This enables principled forecasting of augmentation benefits on training efficiency and downstream task accuracy, a capability absent in prior work such as “Data Augmentation Scaling for Low-Resource Language Model Training,” which does not provide explicit, mathematically grounded scaling laws for augmentation-conditioned training regimes.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a latency-guaranteed, energy-adaptive neuromorphic computing framework that dynamically combines event-driven sparse activation with hardware-aware spike pruning and fine-grained latency-aware scheduling to achieve sub-10ms response times and >90% accuracy on real-time sensory tasks while reducing energy consumption by at least 25% compared to prior event-driven models.

BACKGROUND:  
Current event-driven neuromorphic methods such as Low-latency Event-Driven Sparse Computation optimize for latency under sparse spike conditions but suffer from unpredictable burst-induced delays and lack integrated energy-adaptive control. This results in limited applicability to strictly latency-critical, energy-constrained sensory-motor domains, especially where power budgets are tight. Addressing the open problem of jointly guaranteeing bounded low-latency responses and energy efficiency in real-time neuromorphic sensory processing remains unsolved.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid event-driven neuromorphic framework that fuses a hardware-aware spike pruning mechanism with a latency-aware dynamic scheduler that actively monitors spike burst patterns and selectively throttles neuron activations to maintain latency bounds. The method incorporates an energy-adaptive gating module that regulates neuron firing thresholds based on real-time energy feedback from the hardware substrate, enabling dynamic trade-offs between energy consumption and latency. This layered control ensures end-to-end response latency below 10ms while maintaining classification accuracy above 90%. The system is designed for FPGA and ASIC neuromorphic platforms with real-time sensory inputs.

EXPERIMENT:  
The framework will be evaluated on the N-MNIST, DVS Gesture, and CIFAR10-DVS datasets to benchmark latency, classification accuracy, and energy consumption. Baselines include the original Low-latency Event-Driven Sparse Computation method, the hardware-implemented DropConnect ferroelectric synaptic transistor model, and convolutional neuromorphic network models from 2016. Metrics include achieving >90% accuracy, sub-10ms end-to-end latency, and at least 25% reduction in measured energy per inference compared to Low-latency Event-Driven Sparse Computation. Experiments will run on FPGA neuromorphic platforms with a training compute budget capped at 48 GPU hours, with at least 5 random seeds per condition and ablation studies on spike pruning and energy gating modules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either focuses solely on latency guarantees or hardware-level energy efficiency without integrated latency control, this approach uniquely combines dynamic hardware-aware spike pruning with latency-aware scheduling and energy-adaptive gating to jointly guarantee latency bounds and energy savings. Prior event-driven models do not explicitly incorporate real-time energy feedback for adaptive neuron activation control, making this the first neuromorphic framework to simultaneously address predictable low-latency and energy efficiency in real-time sensory processing.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an integrated neuromorphic computing architecture leveraging hybrid opto-electronic 2D-material synapses combined with on-chip hardware-implemented stochastic DropConnect to achieve sub-femtojoule per synaptic operation energy efficiency while maintaining high learning accuracy and robust generalization.

BACKGROUND:  
Current energy-efficient neuromorphic systems face a trade-off between synaptic device complexity, energy consumption, and learning performance. Although recent advances in 2D-material-based opto-electronic synapses and hardware DropConnect implementations have shown promising energy savings and stochastic regularization respectively, none have combined these two paradigms at the device and architectural level to minimize power without sacrificing accuracy or scalability in realistic tasks.

APPROACH:  
We propose a neuromorphic chip architecture integrating multibit opto-electronic synapses based on ReS2/h-BN/graphene heterostructures with embedded ferroelectric synaptic transistors implementing stochastic DropConnect functionality at the hardware level. This hybrid design enables simultaneous optical and electrical weight modulation for high-precision synaptic updates and in-situ stochastic regularization, reducing overfitting and energy use. The system employs a vertical dual-gate neurotransistor array for short-term memory effects, facilitating temporal coding and further compute-power savings. Training will leverage direct on-chip learning with online weight updates using a custom low-voltage protocol.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on ImageNet-1K for vision classification, SQuAD 2.0 for natural language understanding, and GLUE for robust NLP generalization. Baseline comparisons include the 2016 convolutional neuromorphic network, the 2025 ReS2/h-BN/graphene opto-electronic synapse, and the 2025 ferroelectric DropConnect synaptic transistor. Metrics will focus on classification accuracy improvements (>2% absolute on ImageNet top-1), energy per synaptic operation (<1 fJ target), and training latency reduction (>30% versus baseline architectures). Experiments will run on FPGA-accelerated neuromorphic emulators with three random seeds per setting and ablation studies on synapse type, DropConnect stochasticity level, and memory retention time constants.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that isolates opto-electronic synaptic devices or hardware-implemented DropConnect functions, this research uniquely merges both at the device-to-architecture level, enabling multilevel optical-electrical synaptic updates with embedded stochastic regularization. This synergy addresses the open challenge of jointly maximizing energy-efficiency, learning precision, and system scalability, which no single prior approach has demonstrably achieved on both vision and language benchmarks.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an integrated neuromorphic computing architecture combining ultra-low-power vertical dual-gate neurotransistors with hardware-implemented stochastic DropConnect and multibit optoelectronic synapses to achieve scalable, highly energy-efficient learning and inference.

BACKGROUND:  
Current energy-efficient neuromorphic hardware approaches often optimize individual device types or specific functions but struggle to simultaneously achieve ultra-low energy consumption, scalable stochastic regularization, and multilevel synaptic precision in a single unified system. Existing vertical dual-gate neurotransistors achieve extremely low read power and biologically comparable energy consumption but lack integrated hardware stochasticity for regularization. Conversely, hardware DropConnect implementations provide stochasticity and scalability but do not exploit the ultra-low power or multibit precision of advanced synaptic devices. Bridging these capabilities in a cohesive neuromorphic architecture remains an open challenge critical for advancing energy-efficient, robust learning systems.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid neuromorphic architecture that integrates (1) vertical dual-gate electrolyte-gated neurotransistors with short-term memory for ultra-low power synaptic operations, (2) ferroelectric synaptic transistors supporting hardware-implemented stochastic DropConnect for on-chip regularization, and (3) multibit optoelectronic synapses based on ReS2/h-BN/graphene heterostructures to enable high-precision weight updates via optical programming. The system will leverage the neurotransistors for low-power baseline synaptic activity, the ferroelectric elements to inject controlled stochasticity improving generalization, and optoelectronic synapses to refine synaptic weights with fine granularity and minimal energy overhead. Cross-device interfacing schemes and co-optimized learning protocols will be developed to unify these components into an energy-efficient, scalable spiking neural network platform.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on CIFAR-10, ImageNet-1K, and N-MNIST datasets for visual recognition tasks and NMNIST for spiking data classification. Baselines will include vertical dual-gate neurotransistor-based networks, hardware DropConnect DNNs, and multibit optoelectronic synapse models from prior work. Metrics will measure classification accuracy (target >2% absolute improvement over best prior baseline), energy per inference (<30 fJ per synaptic read/write operation), and robustness via generalization tests (reduce test error variance by ≥15%). Experiments will run on a hardware prototype with energy measurement instrumentation, training with 3 seeds each, and ablation studies isolating each component’s contribution.

NOVELTY:  
This approach meaningfully surpasses prior work by co-integrating ultralow-power neurotransistors, stochastic hardware DropConnect, and multibit optoelectronic synapses within a single platform — a combination not previously realized. Prior works optimize these elements in isolation, but none deliver a scalable neuromorphic computing system with simultaneous gains in energy efficiency, stochastic regularization, and multilevel synaptic precision.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA: Develop EdgeCausNet, a lightweight, streaming-capable causal discovery framework optimized for resource-constrained health monitoring edge devices, combining adaptive nonlinear kernel approximations with incremental structure learning to deliver near real-time, robust causal insights from noisy observational data.

BACKGROUND: Current causal discovery algorithms, including KiNCD and S-FCI, achieve strong accuracy and nonlinear modeling but require substantial computational and memory resources unsuitable for deployment on wearable edge devices with intermittent connectivity and streaming data. The open problem is to create an efficient, low-power causal discovery method that can dynamically update causal models in real-time on edge hardware while handling noise and data drift inherent in physiological data streams.

APPROACH: EdgeCausNet integrates a compressed kernel embedding approach that approximates nonlinear dependencies with minimal memory alongside an incremental causal graph update mechanism based on streaming conditional independence tests and lightweight structure search heuristics. The method includes noise-aware adaptive thresholds and temporal smoothing to mitigate sensor noise and missing data. It runs entirely on-device with periodic opportunistic sync for model refinement, enabling continuous causal discovery under strict power and latency constraints.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate EdgeCausNet on real-world healthcare time-series datasets including MIMIC-III Waveform Database, PhysioNet Challenge 2012, and WESAD wearable stress dataset. Compare against KiNCD, S-FCI, and Orthogonal Structure Search using Structural Hamming Distance (SHD), F1-score for causal edge recovery (targeting >3% absolute improvement in F1), runtime latency (targeting <200ms per update on ARM Cortex-M7 equivalent), and energy consumption metrics (aiming for at least 30% reduction vs baselines). Conduct 5-seed runs with ablations on kernel compression and noise adaptation.

NOVELTY: Unlike KiNCD or S-FCI, EdgeCausNet is explicitly designed for edge deployment on streaming observational data with intermittent connectivity, achieving a novel trade-off between nonlinear causal discovery accuracy and strict resource constraints. Prior work lacks incremental model updates and adaptive noise handling tailored for wearable health monitoring scenarios, which EdgeCausNet addresses with its compressed kernel embeddings and real-time causal graph refinement.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a multi-modal generative AI framework that integrates molecular graph transformers with protein structure-conditioned diffusion models to generate drug-like molecules optimized simultaneously for multi-target binding affinity, ADMET properties, and synthetic accessibility.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative AI approaches for drug discovery either focus on single-target molecular design or rely heavily on sequence-based representations that lack fine-grained 3D structure conditioning, limiting their ability to design highly specific, multi-target therapeutics with favorable pharmacokinetics and ease of synthesis. Existing models also do not jointly optimize molecular generation with diverse biological and chemical constraints, which hampers translational success from in silico to in vitro validation.

APPROACH:  
We propose combining a graph transformer encoder-decoder architecture that generates molecular graphs with a protein structure-conditioned continuous diffusion generative model that refines molecules based on predicted multi-target binding pockets derived from AlphaFold2-predicted protein conformations. The framework incorporates multi-objective reinforcement learning to optimize binding affinities (via docking score surrogates), predicted ADMET profiles (using pre-trained ChemBERTa classifiers), and synthetic accessibility scores during molecule generation. This end-to-end differentiable system allows joint training and fine-tuning using a multi-task loss balancing structural novelty, biological activity, and drug-likeness.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the MOSES benchmark for molecular generation, the PDBBind v2020 dataset for protein-ligand complexes, and the ChEMBL 29 dataset for ADMET annotations. Baseline comparisons include GraphVAE, MolGPT, and DiffGraph. Metrics include validity, novelty, and uniqueness of generated molecules (>90%), docking score improvements (>1 kcal/mol better than baselines), ADMET classification F1-score improvements (>5% absolute), and synthetic accessibility (SA) score reductions (>10% relative). Conduct ablation studies on each conditioning modality and multi-objective components using 3 random seeds on NVIDIA A100 GPUs under a 2-week training budget.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either generates molecules solely from chemical structure or protein sequence, this method uniquely integrates protein 3D structural information via AlphaFold2 with a diffusion-based molecular refinement step, enabling precise multi-target drug design. It also jointly optimizes binding, ADMET, and synthetic accessibility in a unified, end-to-end trainable framework, which no current generative AI approach achieves simultaneously.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
End-to-end differentiable multi-modal generative modeling integrating molecular dynamics trajectories and quantum chemistry outputs within a unified cloud-based architecture to simultaneously optimize molecular structure generation for binding affinity, synthetic accessibility, and multi-target efficacy.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative AI methods for drug design typically incorporate molecular dynamics (MD) simulations and quantum chemistry (QC) calculations separately or apply these simulation results post hoc, limiting their ability to capture synergistic effects between temporal conformational dynamics and electronic structure during molecule generation. This causes suboptimal ranking and design of candidate molecules, particularly when balancing binding affinity with synthetic feasibility and multi-target activity. There is a critical need for a framework that fuses these rich simulation modalities directly into the generative process to enhance screening accuracy and candidate quality at scale.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-modal generative model architecture that jointly encodes sequence-based molecular representations alongside embeddings derived from MD trajectory feature extractors and QC descriptor networks (e.g., learned electronic density features). These inputs are combined via a differentiable fusion module trained end-to-end using cloud-based large batch generation and screening. The model employs a transformer backbone conditioned on both structural and simulation modalities to generate novel molecules optimized with multi-objective reinforcement learning targeting binding affinity, synthetic accessibility score (SAS), and multi-target activity profiles. The entire pipeline leverages cloud GPU clusters for scalable training and inference, incorporating continual self-improvement through active learning from simulation feedback.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will validate on the PDBbind v2020 dataset for protein-ligand binding affinity prediction, QM9 for quantum chemical property generation, and the ChEMBL27 multi-target bioactivity dataset. Baselines include GraphAF, MolGPT, ChemBERTa, and the winning tournament model “Multi-Modal Integration of Simulation Outputs in Cloud-Based Generative Models.” Metrics include >90% molecular validity, >5% absolute improvement in Pearson correlation of predicted vs experimental binding affinities on PDBbind, >10% improvement in synthetic accessibility score distribution, and >8% increase in multi-target activity coverage on ChEMBL27. Experimental protocols specify training on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs for 7 days, with 5 random seeds for robustness and ablation of individual modality contributions.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works that treat molecular dynamics and quantum chemistry data independently or as post-processing filters, this method enables a fully differentiable, end-to-end fusion of temporal and electronic simulation modalities directly within the generative model. This integration captures synergistic interdependencies that improve molecular property prediction and generation quality beyond existing methods, enabling simultaneous optimization of binding affinity, synthetic feasibility, and multi-target drug profiles in a scalable cloud environment.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Develop a highly compressed, privacy-preserving generative AI framework for on-device molecular design tailored to wearable health monitors, enabling real-time, low-power synthesis of personalized drug candidates under strict hardware constraints.

BACKGROUND: Current generative AI models for drug discovery require substantial computational resources and cloud-based processing, limiting their feasibility for integration into wearable health devices. These devices demand ultra-low power consumption, real-time inference, and robust privacy guarantees to generate personalized molecular designs on-device, a capability not addressed by existing large-scale generative AI frameworks.

APPROACH: We propose a novel pipeline combining state-of-the-art model compression techniques—such as quantization-aware training, structured pruning, and knowledge distillation—with lightweight Transformer-based architectures optimized for molecular graph and SMILES generation. This framework integrates federated learning and differential privacy mechanisms to enable personalized molecular design directly on resource-constrained devices like ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers or Snapdragon Wear platforms. Real-time inference is achieved via an optimized runtime leveraging hardware accelerators and approximate computing strategies.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on benchmark datasets including ZINC15 (subset of drug-like molecules), QM9 (quantum chemical properties), and MOSES (molecular generation benchmark). Baselines include GPT-2 fine-tuned for molecular SMILES generation, MolGPT, and GraphVAE. Metrics will measure molecular validity (>95%), novelty (>80%), uniqueness (>90%), generation latency (<100 ms per molecule), power consumption reduction (>50% vs baseline), and privacy leakage quantified by membership inference attack accuracy (<5%). Experiments will use embedded AI development boards (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson Nano, Google Coral) with multiple random seeds (n=5) and ablations on compression levels and privacy parameters.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that focuses predominantly on cloud-based or workstation-scale generative models for drug discovery, this research concretely addresses on-device molecular generation under stringent hardware and privacy constraints, enabling real-time personalized drug candidate synthesis on wearable platforms—a capability currently unexplored and unattainable with existing generative AI methods.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a hierarchical multi-scale deep learning emulator incorporating uncertainty-aware attention mechanisms to accelerate regional climate model (RCM) projections from global climate model (GCM) inputs, explicitly modeling internal variability and improving transferability across climate regimes.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration struggle with internal variability representation, limited transferability to unseen climate regimes, and inconsistent emulation of fine-scale regional climate features, limiting their utility in ensemble projections and scenario analysis. There is a need for an emulator that models both multi-scale spatial-temporal dependencies and uncertainty to robustly accelerate RCM outputs from GCM data while quantifying uncertainty and improving generalization.

APPROACH: We propose a hierarchical deep neural network architecture combining multi-scale spatiotemporal convolutional layers with a novel uncertainty-aware multi-head attention module that explicitly models internal variability patterns via Bayesian dropout and quantile regression. The model inputs coarse GCM outputs and outputs fine-resolution RCM projections, trained end-to-end with a composite loss combining mean squared error, quantile loss, and an uncertainty regularizer. Transfer learning and domain adaptation techniques will be integrated to enhance emulator generalization across different climate regimes and emission scenarios.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the CORDEX regional climate model dataset, the CMIP6 GCM-RCM paired simulations, and the MPI Grand Ensemble data for internal variability benchmarks. Baselines include state-of-the-art models: DeepESM (2023), U-Net with Bayesian quantile regression (2025), and ArrowNet81 (2025). Metrics include spatial-temporal RMSE reduction (>5% improvement over DeepESM), uncertainty calibration error (reduce by 10%), and downscaling transferability accuracy across unseen regions (increase by >7%). Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a 72-hour training budget, reporting averages over 5 seeds and ablation studies isolating uncertainty modeling and attention components.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that either neglects internal variability or treats uncertainty with simplistic methods, this approach explicitly integrates hierarchical uncertainty-aware attention and domain adaptation for robust multi-scale emulation, enabling more accurate and transferable regional downscaling from global model outputs with quantified predictive confidence.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a hierarchical multi-scale deep learning emulator architecture with integrated uncertainty quantification and transfer learning to accelerate climate model simulations while improving regional downscaling accuracy and robustness across scenarios.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning emulators for climate models either lack robustness across different emission scenarios and regions or fail to capture multi-scale spatiotemporal dependencies inherent in climate data, limiting their reliability for ensemble projections and regional downscaling. Moreover, uncertainty quantification remains insufficiently integrated, restricting trust in emulator predictions for decision-making.

APPROACH: We propose a multi-scale hierarchical U-Net inspired architecture that explicitly models global-to-regional climate interactions by fusing coarse global climate model outputs with fine regional climate model features, combined with Bayesian neural network layers to provide calibrated uncertainty estimates. A transfer learning framework will enable adapting pretrained global-to-regional emulators to new regions and scenarios with limited additional data. The model will be trained end-to-end using multi-task objectives combining climate variable reconstruction and uncertainty calibration.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the CORDEX regional climate downscaling dataset, the CMIP6 multi-model ensemble outputs, and the ClimateBench benchmark. Baselines include the original U-Net emulator (as in “Uncertainty Quantification for Surface Ozone Emulators”), DeepESN (Echo State Networks for climate), and classical linear pattern scaling. Metrics: improve RMSE by >10% and CRPS (Continuous Ranked Probability Score) for uncertainty by >15% relative to baselines; reduce inference latency by at least 25% versus a standard RCM run. Perform ablation on hierarchy depth, uncertainty modules, and transfer learning effectiveness across at least 5 different regions/scenarios with 3 random seeds.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that either focuses on single-scale emulation or only adds uncertainty post-hoc, our hierarchical multi-scale architecture jointly models cross-scale climate dependencies with integrated Bayesian uncertainty quantification, enabling accurate and trustworthy emulation of regional climate projections across diverse conditions. The transfer learning component directly addresses transferability limitations noted in recent studies, providing a robust framework for scalable climate model acceleration.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a Latency-Aware Multi-Objective Federated Learning framework that jointly optimizes adaptive client selection, model personalization, and communication compression to achieve faster convergence under heterogeneous network delays and non-IID data distributions, explicitly balancing accuracy, latency, and fairness constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing federated learning (FL) methods with adaptive client selection primarily focus on latency constraints by modeling network and computation heterogeneity but often neglect the compounded challenges of statistical heterogeneity (non-IID data), model personalization needs, and communication overhead in realistic edge networks. Current approaches, such as the winning "Latency-Constrained Federated Learning with Adaptive Client Selection," optimize for latency vs accuracy trade-offs but assume uniform global models and do not integrate communication compression or fairness-aware client inclusion, limiting practical deployment in large-scale, heterogeneous FL systems with strict latency requirements.

APPROACH:  
We propose a Latency-Aware Multi-Objective Federated Learning (LAMO-FL) algorithm that dynamically selects clients each round based on a joint optimization of estimated network latency, data representativeness, and client contribution to personalized model components. LAMO-FL integrates adaptive communication compression (e.g., quantization and sparsification) tailored per-client to reduce uplink latency and bandwidth usage while preserving gradient quality. The method also employs a hierarchical model architecture separating global and personalized layers, enabling partial model aggregation that respects client non-IID data distributions without degrading global model convergence speed. A fairness constraint ensures diverse client participation over time to avoid bias and underrepresentation.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on FEMNIST, CIFAR-10, and Shakespeare datasets to represent vision and NLP tasks with heterogeneous and non-IID client data. Baseline comparisons include the winning Latency-Constrained Federated Learning with Adaptive Client Selection, FedGKD, and Adaptive Dual-Mode Distillation. Metrics tracked are top-1 accuracy (+3% absolute improvement target over baseline), round-trip latency (reduce by ≥30% vs full client participation baseline), communication overhead (reduce total transmitted bits by ≥40%), and fairness indices (e.g., Jain’s index >0.85 for client participation). Experiments run on simulated heterogeneous network environments modeled after 6G IoT edge settings, with 5 random seeds and ablation studies on communication compression and personalized layer impact.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that separately addresses latency, model personalization, or communication efficiency, LAMO-FL jointly optimizes these dimensions with explicit multi-objective client selection and compression tailored to heterogeneous delays and data non-IIDness, achieving faster convergence under strict latency budgets while maintaining accuracy and fairness—capabilities absent in existing adaptive client selection and federated personalization methods.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
A scalable deterministic framework for direct uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks that unifies epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty estimation via an integrated uncertainty embedding layer, enabling accurate, low-overhead uncertainty predictions without Bayesian inference, ensembles, or latent-space sampling.

BACKGROUND:  
Current uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods in deep neural networks either rely on computationally expensive Bayesian approximations (e.g., Bayesian Neural Networks, MC Dropout) or ensembles that hinder scalability, or latent-space inference techniques that fail to jointly capture epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty efficiently. Existing approaches struggle to provide reliable, interpretable uncertainty estimates in high-dimensional parameter spaces, which is critical for safety-sensitive applications such as autonomous systems and medical diagnosis.

APPROACH:  
We propose embedding a novel uncertainty embedding layer directly within the network architecture that deterministically parameterizes uncertainty as learnable, differentiable representations alongside the main predictive features. This layer simultaneously models epistemic uncertainty through a learned sensitivity map of network parameters, and aleatoric uncertainty via heteroscedastic variance modeling, all trained end-to-end with a multi-objective loss combining prediction accuracy and calibrated uncertainty measures. This avoids the overhead of sampling or ensembles, while providing a unified mechanism capturing both uncertainty types with low computational cost.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on ImageNet-1K for large-scale visual classification, CIFAR-100 for medium-scale image recognition with class overlap, and SQuAD 2.0 for natural language understanding with out-of-distribution question detection. Baselines include MC Dropout, Deep Ensembles, and Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC). Metrics measured are Expected Calibration Error (ECE) with a target reduction of >3% absolute compared to baselines, Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) improvement by at least 0.05, and a 30% reduction in inference latency vs Deep Ensembles. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds, including ablations isolating the epistemic and aleatoric components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike PSC and latent-space inference methods that require additional probabilistic modeling or intermediate feature fitting, our approach directly integrates uncertainty estimation into the network’s forward pass as a deterministic embedding, enabling simultaneous, scalable quantification of epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties without sampling or ensembles. This provides both computational efficiency and improved reliability in high-dimensional settings where prior methods fail or incur prohibitive overhead.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a direct probabilistic modeling framework that integrates uncertainty quantification into the training of deep neural networks by jointly learning a predictive distribution over outputs and intermediate representations via hierarchical Bayesian skip connections, enabling scalable, calibrated, and interpretable uncertainty estimates without reliance on ensembles or expensive sampling.

BACKGROUND:  
Current uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods for deep neural networks either require expensive post-hoc sampling (e.g., Monte Carlo Dropout, ensembles) or rely on latent-space heuristics that do not directly model output uncertainty with principled probabilistic treatment. The open problem is to enable direct, scalable, and theoretically grounded uncertainty estimation that captures both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty in a single end-to-end trainable model, while also providing interpretable intermediate uncertainty signals. Existing approaches such as Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC) identify intermediate layers for probabilistic modeling but do not jointly optimize predictive distributions and intermediate uncertainties with hierarchical Bayesian inference.

APPROACH:  
We propose Hierarchical Bayesian Probabilistic Skip Connections (HB-PSC), a novel framework that inserts trainable probabilistic latent variables at multiple intermediate layers of a deep neural network, representing uncertainty at different abstraction levels. These latent variables are governed by hierarchical Bayesian priors enabling joint posterior inference of both intermediate features and final outputs via amortized variational inference. This joint approach learns predictive output distributions and intermediate feature uncertainties concurrently, capturing both epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty in a coherent manner. Training is end-to-end with a variational evidence lower bound (ELBO) objective, allowing scalable uncertainty quantification without ensembles or Monte Carlo sampling at inference.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate HB-PSC on CIFAR-10, ImageNet-1K, and UCI Regression datasets, comparing against Monte Carlo Dropout, Deep Ensembles, and Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC). Use metrics including Expected Calibration Error (ECE < 3%), Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) reduction by >5% absolute, and Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection AUROC improvement by >7% versus baselines. Conduct ablation studies on the number and placement of skip connections and hierarchical prior configurations. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds per setting to ensure statistical validity.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that separately models deterministic features or relies on post-hoc uncertainty approximations, HB-PSC jointly learns a hierarchical Bayesian probabilistic model over intermediate layers and final outputs, enabling direct, scalable, and interpretable end-to-end uncertainty quantification. This approach surpasses PSC by incorporating hierarchical priors and amortized inference that captures richer uncertainty representations and provides calibrated predictive distributions without ensemble overhead or expensive Monte Carlo sampling.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Develop a lightweight Bayesian active subspace uncertainty quantification framework optimized for real-time inference on edge devices with constrained compute and memory, integrating probabilistic latent-space modeling and efficient skip connections.

BACKGROUND: Current uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods in deep neural networks (DNNs) generally require high computational resources and memory, limiting their applicability on edge devices with strict latency and power constraints. Existing approaches like active subspaces and probabilistic skip connections do not explicitly address the trade-off between UQ accuracy and efficiency under intermittent connectivity and real-time demands on edge platforms.

APPROACH: We propose Edge-BASQ (Edge Bayesian Active Subspace Quantification), which fuses active subspace dimensionality reduction of the parameter space with lightweight probabilistic skip connections applied to latent-space features, enabling efficient Bayesian inference with minimal overhead. The framework incorporates a sparse variational approximation to maintain uncertainty estimates while reducing model size and leverages an adaptive inference scheduler that dynamically adjusts UQ computation frequency based on real-time constraints and connectivity status.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate Edge-BASQ on CIFAR-10, ImageNet-1K, and Office-Home datasets for classification tasks deployed on ARM Cortex-A72 edge hardware, comparing against MC Dropout, Deep Ensembles, and the Probabilistic Skip Connections method. Metrics include uncertainty calibration error (ECE) with target <3%, inference latency under 30ms per sample, and memory footprint reduction by at least 40% compared to ensembles. Conduct ablation studies on subspace dimensionality and inference scheduling.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work which either focuses on UQ accuracy (e.g., probabilistic skip connections) or scalable subspace methods without edge constraints, Edge-BASQ explicitly integrates active subspace Bayesian modeling with probabilistic skip connections optimized for stringent edge-device resource budgets and real-time uncertainty estimation, addressing intermittent connectivity and latency dynamically—this combined approach is not demonstrated in current literature.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable multimodal foundation model that integrates paired single-cell genomic, transcriptomic, and epigenomic data using hierarchical contrastive learning and cross-modal attention to produce unified, biologically interpretable embeddings that generalize across tissue types and species.

BACKGROUND:  
Current single-cell foundation models primarily process unimodal data or apply simple multimodal fusion strategies that fail to fully capture complex cross-modal regulatory relationships, limiting interpretability and robustness in diverse biological contexts. This bottleneck impedes tasks such as precise cell-type classification, lineage trajectory inference, and regulatory network reconstruction, especially across heterogeneous datasets spanning multiple tissues and species. There is a critical need for models that jointly represent multi-omic single-cell data with explicit cross-modal interaction modeling and scalable training paradigms.

APPROACH:  
We propose Hierarchical Multimodal Contrastive Attention (HiMCA), a model architecture combining modality-specific encoders (transformers for ATAC-seq, RNA-seq, and methylation data) with a hierarchical contrastive learning objective that aligns representations at cell, modality, and batch levels. Cross-modal attention layers explicitly model regulatory dependencies between modalities, enhancing interpretability by identifying key cross-omic feature interactions. Training employs curriculum learning starting from unimodal pretraining to progressive multimodal integration with hard negative mining. The model is optimized end-to-end using a hybrid contrastive plus reconstruction loss to preserve modality-specific information while enforcing unified embedding space coherence.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate HiMCA on three benchmark paired single-cell multi-omic datasets: SHARE-seq (joint chromatin accessibility and transcriptomics), SNARE-seq (paired scRNA-seq and scATAC-seq), and scNMT-seq (simultaneous measurement of DNA methylation, chromatin accessibility, and transcriptomics). Baselines include Multimodal Contrastive Learning (the tournament winner), TotalVI, and Multigrate. Metrics will include >3% absolute improvement in cell-type classification F1-score, >5% increase in cross-modal batch effect correction silhouette score, and >10% improvement in regulatory motif recovery AUC relative to baselines. Training will be performed on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of ~1M GPU hours, running each experiment with 5 random seeds and ablations on contrastive loss components and attention layers.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike existing multimodal foundation models that rely on flat contrastive objectives or unimodal embeddings, HiMCA introduces hierarchical contrastive learning and explicit cross-modal attention to capture biologically meaningful regulatory interactions, enabling superior generalization across tissues and species. This approach substantially enhances interpretability and downstream analytic power, fulfilling unmet needs in scalable, multimodal single-cell representation learning.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a cross-tissue, multimodal, scalable single-cell foundation model leveraging contrastive learning and graph-based representations to robustly integrate genomic, transcriptomic, and spatial data, enabling improved interpretability and generalization across diverse human populations.

BACKGROUND:  
Current single-cell foundation models like Nephrobase Cell+ focus on organ-specific or species-specific data, limiting their applicability across tissues and populations. Additionally, existing models inadequately integrate multimodal single-cell data (e.g., RNA-seq, ATAC-seq, and spatial transcriptomics) in a unified representation, and they struggle to generalize robustly across diverse human genetic backgrounds and environmental contexts. This hinders comprehensive insights into cellular states and disease mechanisms on a global scale.

APPROACH:  
We propose a single-cell foundation model architecture that combines graph neural networks (GNNs) to encode cellular neighborhood and gene regulatory interactions, with a contrastive multimodal learning framework aligning transcriptomic, epigenomic, and spatial modalities. The model is pretrained on a large, diverse compendium of single-cell datasets encompassing multiple tissues (e.g., Tabula Sapiens, Human Cell Atlas [HCA], and HuBMAP) and population backgrounds to learn a global, transferable cellular representation. A dedicated interpretability module based on attention mechanisms will highlight modality-specific and cross-modal feature importance, facilitating biological insight extraction.

EXPERIMENT:  
Train and evaluate on the Tabula Sapiens v2, Human Cell Atlas (HCA) bone marrow dataset, and HuBMAP kidney spatial transcriptomics dataset. Baselines include Nephrobase Cell+, scGPT, and scBERT. Evaluation metrics:  
- Cell type classification accuracy (>5% absolute improvement over Nephrobase Cell+ on cross-tissue tasks)  
- Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) for clustering consistency (>0.1 absolute gain)  
- Cross-modal retrieval precision@10 (>10% relative increase)  
- Interpretability quantified by agreement with known marker gene sets (>15% improvement in precision over attention baselines)  

Training will use 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs over 4 weeks, with results averaged over 5 random seeds. Ablations will test the contribution of each modality and the graph component.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that is either organ-specific or uni-modal, this approach explicitly integrates multiple single-cell modalities and diverse tissues within a single scalable foundation model, enabling transfer across biological contexts and populations. The use of graph-based contrastive multimodal learning and an interpretability module addresses current limitations in generalization and mechanistic insight, unattainable by existing models like Nephrobase Cell+ or scGPT.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable multimodal foundation model architecture that integrates single-cell transcriptomics, epigenomics, and spatial genomics data into a unified embedding space, enabling accurate cross-modal cell type annotation, gene regulatory network inference, and cross-species transfer learning with interpretable biological representations.

BACKGROUND:  
Current foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology lack the ability to jointly model multiple single-cell modalities (e.g., scRNA-seq, scATAC-seq, spatial transcriptomics) at scale, limiting their capacity to capture complex biological relationships and heterogeneity across cell types and species. This absence of a unified, interpretable model hinders comprehensive biological insight, accurate cell type annotation, and robust cross-species generalization.

APPROACH:  
We propose a transformer-based multimodal architecture with modality-specific encoders fused via a shared latent embedding space using cross-attention layers, trained with contrastive and reconstruction objectives tailored to each modality. We incorporate hierarchical biological priors such as gene regulatory networks and spatial neighborhood graphs to guide attention mechanisms and improve interpretability. The model is pretrained on integrated multi-omics single-cell datasets across human and mouse tissues and fine-tuned for downstream tasks like cell type classification and gene regulatory network inference.

EXPERIMENT:  
Train and evaluate on the Human Cell Atlas (HCA) single-cell RNA-seq and ATAC-seq paired datasets, the 10x Genomics Multiome PBMC dataset, and the Spatial Transcriptomics Benchmark (STAB). Compare against baselines including scBERT, SCALEX, and Nephrobase Cell+. Metrics include >3% absolute improvement in cell type annotation F1-score, >15% increase in gene regulatory network inference AUPR, and >10% accuracy gain in cross-species cell type transfer learning. Conduct ablations on modality fusion strategies, biological prior integration, and embedding dimensionality. Experiments will use 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs over 5 training runs with fixed seeds.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior single-modal or unimodal foundation models (e.g., scBERT or Nephrobase Cell+), our method jointly models multiple single-cell modalities and spatial data within a biologically-informed transformer architecture that enables interpretable, scalable embeddings and robust cross-species transfer—capabilities absent in current state-of-the-art models.
