topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Introduce a hierarchical multi-modal emergent communication framework combining discrete neuro-spike-inspired signaling with continuous learned embeddings to enable scalable, interpretable, and energy-efficient goal-oriented communication in heterogeneous multi-agent systems.

BACKGROUND: Current emergent communication approaches in multi-agent systems either rely on discrete symbolic messages or continuous vector embeddings, but fail to simultaneously achieve energy efficiency, interpretability, and scalability across heterogeneous agents and complex environments. Specifically, existing neuro-spike communication methods minimize bandwidth but do not integrate with goal-oriented continuous semantics, while goal-oriented communication surveys highlight a lack of unified frameworks that simultaneously address efficiency, interpretability, and heterogeneity in real-world multi-agent tasks.

APPROACH: We propose a two-tier communication protocol where low-level discrete neuro-spike signaling (binary single-bit pulses inspired by A Note on Emergent Behavior in Multi-agent Systems Enabled by Neuro-spike Communication) encodes high-level attention and synchronization triggers, while a learned continuous embedding layer encodes semantic content of messages conditioned on agent roles and goals. This hierarchical design enables agents to dynamically allocate bandwidth and optimize communication cost versus semantic richness. The system is trained end-to-end via multi-agent reinforcement learning with inductive biases for positive signaling and listening, augmented by an energy cost regularizer to encourage sparse neuro-spike usage while preserving task performance.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE), StarCraft Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC), and Hanabi benchmark datasets, measuring task success rate, communication bandwidth (bits per timestep), and interpretability via mutual information between signal tiers and agent actions. Baselines include independent Q-learning (IQL), MADDPG with emergent communication, and the neuro-spike communication protocol from the 2025 paper. We aim for >5% absolute improvement in task success rate over neuro-spike baselines, a >30% reduction in communication bandwidth compared to continuous-only protocols, and demonstrable interpretability via cluster analysis of discrete signal patterns. Experiments will be run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds per setup, including ablations on hierarchical vs. flat signaling and energy cost regularization.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work, this framework uniquely integrates neuro-spike discrete signaling with continuous semantic embeddings in a hierarchical architecture that enables both energy-efficient synchronization and rich goal-oriented communication, addressing heterogeneity and scalability—features neither the purely neuro-spike nor the continuous emergent communication methods achieve alone.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA: Develop a lightweight, incremental topological data analysis framework optimized for real-time, streaming high-dimensional IoT sensor data on edge devices with strict computational and memory constraints.

BACKGROUND: Current topological data analysis (TDA) techniques such as Mapper and Ball Mapper require batch processing with substantial memory and compute resources, making them infeasible for deployment on resource-limited edge IoT devices that generate continuous high-dimensional data streams. Existing nonlinear algebraic topology frameworks and TDA applications focus on offline analysis, lacking real-time adaptability and efficiency for edge computing scenarios where latency and power consumption are critical.

APPROACH: We propose an edge-optimized streaming TDA pipeline that incrementally constructs and updates topological summaries using lightweight approximation algorithms and sparse data structures tailored for edge hardware (e.g., ARM Cortex-M processors). The method incorporates a memory-bounded, sliding-window Ball Mapper variant combined with incremental clustering heuristics to track topological features while minimizing recomputation. To optimize power usage, the algorithm dynamically adapts fidelity based on observed data variability and available resources, enabling real-time anomaly detection and feature extraction from multi-modal IoT sensor streams.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on real-world high-dimensional streaming sensor datasets such as the UCI Gas Sensor Array Drift Dataset, Intel Lab Data for environmental sensing, and the PAMAP2 Physical Activity Monitoring dataset. Compare against batch Mapper, Ball Mapper, and the streaming TDA framework from recent work (e.g. “Nonlinear Algebraic Topology” 2025) on metrics including processing latency per data window (<100 ms target), memory footprint (fit within 1 MB RAM), power consumption (measured on representative ARM Cortex-M microcontroller development boards), and topological feature stability and accuracy (quantified by persistence diagram similarity and detection of known events). Target >30% latency reduction and >40% memory savings vs baselines with no more than 5% degradation in topological feature accuracy.

NOVELTY: Unlike existing batch or high-resource TDA methods, this approach is the first to deliver fully incremental, adaptive topological summaries explicitly designed for edge IoT devices with strict resource constraints and real-time demands, enabling practical deployment in IoT sensor networks rather than offline analysis only.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a real-time, hierarchical layer-and-subcomponent attribution framework for transformer models that dynamically adapts attribution granularity under strict latency constraints, enabling interactive mechanistic interpretability with fidelity comparable to offline methods.

BACKGROUND:  
Current mechanistic interpretability methods for transformers either provide detailed, high-fidelity attributions at the cost of high computational latency or offer coarse attributions that enable low-latency analysis but sacrifice explanation quality. The open problem is to achieve fine-grained mechanistic layer attribution—down to attention heads and MLP subcomponents—with rigorously optimized latency below 100ms per layer, to support interactive debugging during model development and deployment. Existing techniques like Latency-Constrained Layer Attribution (LCLA) do not dynamically adjust attribution fidelity based on runtime budgets nor provide hierarchical explanations that balance comprehensiveness and speed.

APPROACH:  
We propose Hierarchical Latency-Aware Attribution (HLAA), a multi-stage attribution pipeline that first performs fast coarse attributions to identify candidate influential layers, heads, and MLP directions, then selectively refines these attributions at finer granularity levels based on an adaptive latency budget scheduler. HLAA leverages a learned lightweight predictor to estimate attribution importance per component and dynamically prunes low-impact components early, reducing unnecessary computation. It integrates singular vector decomposition of MLP activations (extending Beyond Components: Singular Vector-Based Interpretability) to enable subcomponent attribution without significant overhead. Attribution fidelity is evaluated online, and the system adjusts the depth and granularity of analysis to maintain strict per-layer latency below 100ms on commodity GPUs.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate HLAA on GPT-2 (117M) and GPT-Neo (125M) models using Wikitext-103 and the Penn Treebank datasets for language modeling, and on GLUE benchmark tasks for classification interpretability. Baselines include the original Latency-Constrained Layer Attribution (LCLA), Singular Vector-Based Interpretability, and standard layer-wise attribution methods like Integrated Gradients and Attention Rollout. Metrics include attribution fidelity measured by explanation accuracy (>95% correlation with full attribution), latency per layer (<100ms target), and downstream utility quantified by improved mechanistic debugging speed (measured via user study), expecting at least 30% latency reduction over LCLA with less than 5% fidelity loss. Experimental protocol includes 5 random seeds, ablation studies on latency-budget scheduler components, and evaluation on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a total compute budget of 1,000 GPU hours.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike LCLA which statically balances attribution fidelity and latency, HLAA introduces a dynamic, hierarchical, and adaptive attribution mechanism that selectively refines explanations based on real-time latency feedback, enabling fine-grained mechanistic insights at interactive speeds. It combines singular vector decomposition within the latency-constrained framework, a capability absent in prior works, thus providing subcomponent attribution under strict time budgets.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA: Develop a memory- and compute-efficient mechanistic interpretability framework for transformer models that enables fast, partial circuit analysis and causal probing under strict resource constraints on edge devices.

BACKGROUND: Existing mechanistic interpretability (MI) techniques for transformers require full model access and extensive compute, making them infeasible for deployment on edge devices with limited memory and processing power. This gap prevents transparency and trustworthy deployment of transformer models in critical real-world edge scenarios such as mobile NLP and IoT sensor analysis, where interpretability is essential but compute budgets are severely restricted.

APPROACH: We propose a lightweight MI pipeline combining (1) selective subcircuit extraction guided by dynamic importance sampling of attention heads and MLP units using low-cost heuristics, (2) sketching-based approximation of internal activations and gradients that reduces memory footprint and compute, and (3) a causal probing module leveraging sparse interventions on these compact representations to reveal mechanistic roles. This hybrid approach bypasses the need for full model state tracking and enables iterative, fast interpretability queries with bounded resource use, targeting transformer models fine-tuned for edge tasks.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on GLUE (SST-2, MNLI) for NLP edge classification, ImageNet-1K for lightweight vision transformers (ViT-Tiny), and UEA Time Series Classification archive datasets for time series transformers; use BERT-base, ViT-Tiny, and Transformer-Tiny baselines. Measure interpretability quality via fidelity of causal interventions (>85% fidelity), runtime latency reduction (>50% vs full-model MI), and memory usage reduction (>60%). Conduct ablations on subcircuit selection thresholds and sketching parameters. Experiments run on ARM Cortex-A76 edge-class CPUs with 4GB RAM, averaged over 5 seeds.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work which assumes full model availability and large compute budgets (e.g., singular vector decompositions or neuroscience-inspired full residual stream analyses), our method explicitly targets the edge scenario by combining approximate, partial circuit analysis and causal probing under tight resource constraints, enabling practical MI deployment where existing SOTA approaches cannot operate.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA:  
Introduce a Transformer-based latent dynamics model conditioned on abstract task embeddings learned via contrastive meta-pretraining to enable zero-shot generalization across unseen environments with varying dynamics and reward structures in reinforcement learning.

BACKGROUND:  
Current zero-shot generalization (ZSG) methods in reinforcement learning (RL) struggle to simultaneously adapt to unseen changes in both environment dynamics and reward functions without direct context supervision or retraining. Existing techniques either rely heavily on explicit contextual inference (e.g., environment parameters) or pessimistic policy updates that do not leverage transferable latent structure across tasks, limiting their ability to generalize to out-of-distribution tasks with complex dynamics and goals.

APPROACH:  
We propose Contrastive Latent Dynamics Meta-Pretraining (CLDMP), which pretrains a Transformer-based latent dynamics model over a diverse set of source tasks using a contrastive loss that encourages encoding task-invariant and task-discriminative embeddings. During downstream zero-shot evaluation, the pretrained latent model provides abstract task embeddings to condition a policy network, enabling it to infer unseen environment dynamics and reward functions without explicit context input or fine-tuning. This approach integrates latent dynamics modeling and policy learning end-to-end, bridging the gap between contextual inference and robust policy optimization.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate CLDMP on the Meta-World benchmark (ML1 and ML10) for zero-shot multi-task robotic manipulation, the Procgen benchmark for zero-shot generalization to novel procedurally-generated levels, and DeepMind Control Suite with diverse physics variations. Compare against baselines including Contextual Policies with inferred context embeddings (e.g., Inferring Behavior-Specific Context), Pessimistic Proximal Policy Optimization (PPPO), and Equilibrium Policy Generalization (EPG). Metrics include zero-shot task success rate (>10% absolute improvement over baselines), average return (>15% improvement), and generalization gap reduction (<5% difference between training and unseen tasks). Report results over 5 random seeds with ablation on contrastive loss and latent embedding dimension.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods that require explicit contextual signals or rely solely on pessimistic policy updates, CLDMP leverages a pretrained latent dynamics model with contrastive meta-learning to extract transferable abstract task embeddings, enabling simultaneous zero-shot generalization to both unseen dynamics and reward structures without explicit context inputs or fine-tuning. This integrated latent-based conditioning for policy learning is not addressed in existing SOTA approaches.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA:  
Learn disentangled, hierarchical task embeddings that separate universal dynamics from task-specific reward factors, enabling zero-shot generalization via compositional adaptation in meta-reinforcement learning.

BACKGROUND:  
Current meta-RL methods with task embeddings (e.g., Meta-RL with Task Embeddings) capture holistic task representations but struggle to generalize zero-shot when underlying environment dynamics and reward structures vary independently. Prior work does not explicitly model or disentangle these factors, limiting zero-shot transfer to tasks where both dynamics and rewards shift in a coupled, smooth manner. This leaves open the problem of zero-shot adaptation to tasks with compositional variations in environment physics and objectives.

APPROACH:  
We propose Hierarchical Disentangled Task Embeddings (HiDTE), a meta-RL framework that learns two separate embedding spaces: one encoding environment dynamics (transition model factors) and one encoding reward/task objectives. A meta-learner infers both embeddings from minimal interactions via a dual encoder architecture with disentanglement losses (e.g., total correlation minimization and mutual information constraints). The policy conditions on the concatenation of these embeddings and leverages a learnable compositional fusion module to adapt zero-shot to novel tasks formed by unseen combinations of dynamics and reward factors. During training, task variations explicitly manipulate dynamics and reward independently, encouraging disentanglement.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on Meta-World benchmarks (ML10, ML45) and the Procgen environments, testing zero-shot generalization to novel tasks with unseen dynamics-reward combinations. Baselines include PEARL, VariBAD, and the original Meta-RL with Task Embeddings. Metrics: achieve >5% absolute improvement in zero-shot success rate on held-out tasks, reduce adaptation steps by 50% in few-shot settings, and improve task embedding disentanglement metrics (e.g., MIG score). Protocol involves training on 3 seeds per environment, with ablations on disentanglement losses and embedding dimensionality, using 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs over 2 weeks.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods that learn monolithic task embeddings, HiDTE explicitly disentangles dynamics and reward embeddings and leverages their compositional nature, enabling zero-shot generalization to a combinatorial space of unseen task variations, which existing meta-RL frameworks cannot handle due to entangled and holistic task representations.
